concept_name: Wheatstone Bridge — Balance Condition P/Q = R/S
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Ch.3 Current Electricity (§3.14 Wheatstone Bridge) — table-of-contents + scope reference only; teaching authored from first principles. DC Pandey consulted for chapter SCOPE only (Current Electricity ToC places the Wheatstone bridge after Kirchhoff's rules and before the meter bridge / potentiometer) to confirm the atomic boundary — no teaching method, example problem, or figure imported. The atomic idea: four resistors P, Q, R, S arranged in a diamond, a battery across one diagonal (A–C) and a galvanometer across the other (B–D), is BALANCED when P/Q = R/S, because then the two midpoints B and D sit at the SAME potential, so the galvanometer branch carries ZERO current and the needle sits dead centre — a null, not a magnitude — even though all four arms keep flowing; and because the null depends only on the resistance RATIO, never the battery, an unknown resistance reads off the three knowns via S = R·(Q/P) with no current or voltage ever measured. Does NOT cover the meter bridge (1 m wire + jockey, its own diamond), the potentiometer (c23, downstream sibling null method), galvanometer internals or conversion (shipped moving_coil_galvanometer / galvanometer_to_ammeter_voltmeter — here it is a given null-detector), bridge sensitivity / best-arm-ratio / unbalanced-bridge Thevenin current (drill-down candidates only, deferred), or the deflection-mode strain-gauge bridge (real-world-anchor framing only, never a guided state). TENTH Ch.3 concept on the particle_field (2D p5.js) renderer, same engine family as drift_velocity/ohms_law/resistivity/combination_of_resistors/emf_definition/internal_resistance/electrical_power_in_resistor/kirchhoff_junction_rule_KCL/kirchhoff_loop_rule_KVL. Reuses scenario_type 'combination_of_resistors' as the renderer selector (per the approved design's directive to NOT mint a new scenario_type) — but rides engine additions AUTHORED here and FLAGGED for peter_parker:renderer_primitives (via quality_auditor FAIL-routing), NOT yet implemented as of this authoring pass: (1) a new 'bridge' topology value in circuitMode's cTopology() (currently 'series'|'parallel' only) — a diamond layout with node A left / node C right / node B top / node D bottom, four arm boxes P/Q/R/S on the four edges, the battery on the outer A–C loop, and the galvanometer branch on the B–D chord, gated behind topology:'bridge' so combination_of_resistors.json/kirchhoff_junction_rule_KCL.json render pixel-identically when the flag is absent; (2) drawGalvanometerC — a NEW centre-zero dial primitive, needle angle = clamp(25·(V_D−V_B), −60, +60) degrees (calibration constants documented in particle_field_config.bridge_calibration, renderer-tunable if the checkpoints hold: S2 V_D−V_B=−1V → −25°, S3-end/S4 0V → 0° dead centre), gated behind show_galvanometer; (3) two live value-only node readouts (V_B, V_D), sibling to the shipped node_label/kcl_sum_readout pattern, gated behind show_node_readouts; (4) a value-only ratio HUD ('P/Q = 0.50 vs R/S = 1.00 ✗' → '✓'), comparing |P/Q − R/S| against a RATIO_TOL of 0.01 (never strict equality — required for the S5 explore sandbox, where an off-grid R may not hit the exact null), gated behind show_ratio_hud; (5) symbolic bridge-wire beads flowing B→D at a density/direction driven by sign(V_D−V_B) (a deliberate visualization proxy, not a literal high-galvanometer-resistance current — pre-authorized fallback: if fiddly, the needle + node readouts alone carry the pedagogy), gated behind show_bridge_wire_beads; (6) a live value-only unknown-resistance readout ('S = 6.0 Ω'), gated behind show_s_readout, separate from the ONE symbolic formula surface per Rule 34b; (7) a literal 'i_g = 0' value-only label, gated behind show_ig_zero_label, shown ONLY when the live |P/Q − R/S| is genuinely inside RATIO_TOL (STATE_3's scripted balance, and STATE_5's explore sandbox if the teacher nulls it) — never fabricated away from balance; (8) two new clock-driven one-shot sweeps, bridge_r_sweep/bridge_r_sweep_to/bridge_r_sweep_start_ms/bridge_r_sweep_duration_ms and the emf sibling bridge_emf_sweep/bridge_emf_sweep_to/bridge_emf_sweep_start_ms/bridge_emf_sweep_duration_ms, mirroring the shipped r2_autosweep/r3_draw_in pattern but under distinct names (this concept's arms are P/Q/R/S, not R1/R2/R3) and carrying explicit start/duration ms so STATE_3's R 6→3Ω sweep completes in exactly 1400 ms (<1500 ms DEFAULT_REVEAL_MS, keeping the PRIMARY-aha frozen frame settled without touching the often-raced deriveStateMeta.ts whitelist — KVL precedent). Idealization (physics-author constraint, load-bearing): the galvanometer is treated as high-resistance, so away from balance the arm currents i1/i2 use the simple open-circuit divider formulas (exact only in that limit — the true unbalanced bridge-wire current needs a full network solve, deferred to a drill-down candidate); AT balance, i_g = 0 is EXACT for ANY galvanometer resistance, since V_B = V_D forces zero current through whatever sits between them by Ohm's law alone. Zero regression declared to combination_of_resistors/kirchhoff_junction_rule_KCL/kirchhoff_loop_rule_KVL/emf_definition/internal_resistance/electrical_power_in_resistor — every new flag is opt-in (topology:'bridge') and absent from all six sibling JSONs. Conceptual-only (no mode_overrides), EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches) — the two genuine misconceptions ('current stops everywhere at balance', 'you need the battery voltage or a meter reading to find the unknown') are confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch, fired at the STATE_3 and STATE_4 pivots respectively (founder guardrail 2026-07-04: no per-state tic), no predict-pause. Anchors authored UNIVERSAL per Rule 35 (a digital weighing scale's internal strain-gauge bridge — honest deflection-mode framing surfaced only as anchor context, never the sim's own teaching instance, which stays the null-method unknown-resistor measurement; secondary: temperature/pressure sensor bridges — no country, brand, festival, or currency; plain English throughout, no Hinglish). First of the founder's lab trio (Wheatstone c20 → potentiometer c23 → meter bridge, T4 lab nano).

real_world_anchor.primary: A digital weighing scale hides this exact bridge inside it. A strain gauge — a resistor whose value shifts a hair when the platform flexes under your weight — sits as one arm. Stepping on unbalances the bridge, and that tiny imbalance becomes the weight reading. The bridge is how electronics detect a hair-thin resistance change that no ordinary meter could see.
real_world_anchor.secondary: The same bridge sits behind many sensor readouts — temperature and pressure sensors turn a tiny resistance shift into a signal the same way.

P.name: Ratio arm P (upper-left, A–B)
P.role: FIXED 10Ω on all guided states S1–S4 (the ratio arms establish the 1:2 asymmetry deliberately); live slider ONLY in S5 explore

Q.name: Ratio arm Q (upper-right, B–C)
Q.role: FIXED 20Ω on all guided states S1–S4; live slider ONLY in S5 explore

R.name: Known variable arm (lower-left, A–D)
R.role: the ONE taught control on S2/S3 — S1 entry 3 (balanced home pose), S2 lands 6 (post-nudge, live), S3 entry 6 → sweep to 3 (post-reveal live), S4 fixed 3 (background), live S5

S.name: Unknown arm (lower-right, D–C)
S.role: FIXED 6Ω on all guided states S1–S4 (the thing R's balance recovers); live slider ONLY in S5 explore

emf.name: Cell EMF (ideal, ε)
emf.role: locked 6V on S1–S3; the ONE taught control on S4 (entry 3 → sweep to 10, post-reveal live); live S5

i1.name: Upper-path current (P then Q)
i1.role: arm-bead-density driver; INVARIANT 0.2A across S1–S3 (depends only on P,Q,emf) — deliberate, do not 'fix'

i2.name: Lower-path current (R then S)
i2.role: arm-bead-density driver; the ONLY current that responds to R in S2/S3

ratio_gap.name: Balance-ratio mismatch
ratio_gap.role: drives ratio HUD ✓/✗ — compared against RATIO_TOL, never strict equality

needle_deg.name: Galvanometer needle angle
needle_deg.role: live tracking indicator (33d); calibration K_NEEDLE=25 °/V, NEEDLE_MAX_DEG=60°; checkpoints: S2 (VD-VB=-1V)→-25°, S3-end/S4 (0V)→0° dead centre

formulas.balance_condition: P/Q === R/S  <=>  VB === VD  <=>  i_g = 0 (exact, for ANY galvanometer resistance)
formulas.S_unknown: R * (Q / P) — surfaced at S4; contains no emf term by construction
formulas.needle_deg: clamp(25 * (VD - VB), -60, 60) — suggested calibration, tunable; checkpoints: S2 VD-VB=-1V -> -25deg, S3-end/S4 0V -> 0deg

constraints: current_through_galvanometer_zero_at_balance — i_g = 0 EXACTLY at P/Q = R/S, true for ANY finite galvanometer resistance (not merely the high-R idealization)
bridge_arms_show_ratios — balance is a RATIO match (P/Q = R/S), never all four equal; P=10Ω ≠ Q=20Ω deliberate (cluster balance_needs_equal_resistors)
both_arms_flow_always — i1, i2 > 0 in EVERY state including balance; only the BRIDGE-WIRE current is ever zero
needle_centre_zero — needle_deg = 0 is the visual null; dial is centre-zero
balance_is_emf_independent — VB = VD = (2/3)·emf at EVERY emf (P=10,Q=20,R=3,S=6); S = R·(Q/P) has no emf term
i1_invariant_S1_to_S3 — i1 = 0.2A identically on S1/S2/S3 — DELIBERATE
ratio_hud_needs_tolerance — ✓/✗ MUST use |P/Q − R/S| < RATIO_TOL (0.01), never strict equality; guided combos satisfy equality exactly, S5 explore needs the tolerance

explore_null_not_always_reachable — R integer-stepped [1,20]; for arbitrary S5 P,Q,S the exact null R=S·(Q/P) may fall off-grid — EXPECTED honest sandbox behavior (real decade boxes too), never a forced false ✓
no_divide_by_zero — P,Q,R,S floors keep P+Q, R+S, Q/P, S/R finite/nonzero

aha_moment.statement: The needle stops at zero, a null, not a magnitude, because the ratios match.

STATE_1.title: Topology: one battery, two divider paths, one meter across the midpoints

scene_composition.text: One battery drives current into node A, splitting it into two paths.
The upper path runs resistor P then resistor Q; the lower path runs resistor R then resistor S; both recombine at node C.
A meter bridges the two midpoints in between, node B and node D.

tts_sentences.text_en: One battery drives current into node A, splitting it into two paths — through resistor P then resistor Q on top, through resistor R then resistor S below.
Both paths recombine at node C, and a meter bridges the midpoints in between, node B and node D.

STATE_2.title: The galvanometer is a difference detector: unequal ratios swing the needle

scene_composition.text: Resistor R climbs from three ohms to six.
The top midpoint sits at four volts, the bottom at three — they disagree, so current flows in the meter branch and the needle swings off centre.
The ratio on top no longer matches the ratio on the bottom — the bridge is unbalanced.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch resistor R climb from three ohms to six.
The top midpoint now sits at four volts, the bottom at three — they disagree, so current flows in the meter branch and the needle swings off centre.
The ratio on top no longer matches the ratio on the bottom — the bridge is unbalanced.

STATE_3.title: PRIMARY aha — matching the ratios silences the bridge wire; the arms keep flowing

scene_composition.text: Resistor R falls back from six ohms to three.
The bottom midpoint climbs to meet the top at four volts, the meter branch empties, and the needle settles dead at the centre — a true zero.
Every arm keeps carrying current — only the meter branch itself goes silent.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now watch resistor R fall from six ohms back to three.
The ratios match, the bottom midpoint climbs to meet the top at four volts, and the needle settles dead at the centre — a true zero, not a small number.
Yet every arm keeps carrying current — only the meter branch itself goes silent.

misconception_watch.belief: At balance the current stops flowing everywhere.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The bridge-wire beads drain to none and the needle stops dead — while the four arm bead streams keep flowing at 0.2 amperes and 0.67 amperes.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Balance silences only the bridge wire — the arms keep carrying current; the two midpoints simply agree.

STATE_4.title: Why it's precise — S = R·(Q/P) survives any battery

scene_composition.text: The battery's voltage slides from three volts up to ten.
Both midpoints climb together, always equal, and the needle never leaves zero.
The unknown resistor works out to six ohms from the known arm and the ratio of the other two arms — no battery voltage anywhere in the formula.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now slide the battery's voltage from three volts up to ten.
Both midpoints climb together, always equal, and the needle never leaves zero.
That's why the unknown resistor works out to six ohms — the known arm times a ratio of two other arms — with no battery voltage in the formula anywhere.

misconception_watch.belief: You must read the meter or know the battery voltage to get the resistance.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The battery voltage sweeps from three to ten volts, the arm currents speed up and slow, both midpoints climb in lockstep, and the needle never leaves zero — the readout S equals R times Q over P contains no battery term.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The null depends only on the resistance ratio — the battery cancels out, so the unknown reads off the knowns.

STATE_5.title: Explore — every arm and the battery are yours: null it yourself

scene_composition.text: Every arm and the battery are live now — drag any dial.
Unbalance the bridge, then null it again by matching the ratios.
The needle, the readouts, and the ratio all respond live as you drag.

tts_sentences.text_en: Every arm and the battery are yours now — drag any dial, unbalance the bridge, and null it again by matching the ratios.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered the Wheatstone bridge when they can (1) describe the topology — one battery across two arms, current splitting at node A into two divider paths (resistor P then resistor Q, and resistor R then resistor S) that recombine at node C, with the galvanometer bridging the midpoints B and D; (2) explain that the galvanometer is a DIFFERENCE detector — it deflects whenever the two midpoints disagree, however small the difference, not a magnitude meter; (3) state the balance condition P/Q = R/S and recognize that at balance the galvanometer reads exactly zero because the two midpoints reach the same potential, even though all four arms keep carrying current — only the bridge-wire current is zero; (4) apply S = R·(Q/P) to find an unknown resistance from three known ones at balance; and (5) explain why the null and the calculated S are independent of the battery's voltage — the battery cancels out of the ratio, which is exactly why a null method is more precise than reading a meter.

questions.stem: In a Wheatstone bridge, current leaves the battery, enters node A, and can travel through either resistor P then resistor Q, or resistor R then resistor S, before both paths meet again at node C. What is the galvanometer actually connected across?

options.A: The two midpoints, node B and node D — comparing the potential at one to the potential at the other
options.B: The total current leaving the battery at node A
options.C: Just the upper arm, in series with resistor P
options.D: The battery directly, across node A and node C

distractor_misconceptions.B: confuses the galvanometer's bridge-wire position, between the two midpoints, with a main-line ammeter measuring the total current at the junction
distractor_misconceptions.C: misplaces the galvanometer inside a single arm instead of across the two midpoints it is meant to compare
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses the galvanometer branch with a voltmeter placed straight across the source

questions.tested_idea: The galvanometer bridges the two midpoints B and D, not any single arm and not the source itself.
questions.parallel_form_stem: In a Wheatstone bridge, describe exactly which two points in the circuit the galvanometer is connected between, and explain why those two points, rather than any single arm, are the ones worth comparing.

questions.stem: In a bridge with resistor P = 10 Ω, resistor Q = 20 Ω, resistor R = 6 Ω, resistor S = 6 Ω and a 6 V battery, the top midpoint sits at 4.0 V and the bottom midpoint sits at 3.0 V. What does the galvanometer do?

options.A: It deflects, because the two midpoints are at different potentials, so current flows between them
options.B: It stays at zero, because 4.0 V and 3.0 V are close enough
options.C: It reads the full 6 V of the battery
options.D: It deflects because resistor R is bigger than resistor P, regardless of the actual midpoint voltages

distractor_misconceptions.B: treats 'close' potentials as good enough for a null, when the galvanometer detects ANY nonzero difference, however small
distractor_misconceptions.C: confuses the galvanometer branch, which reads a potential DIFFERENCE, with a voltmeter reading the full source
distractor_misconceptions.D: swaps the true cause — a potential difference between the midpoints — for a size comparison between two unrelated resistors

questions.tested_idea: The galvanometer deflects whenever the two midpoints disagree, however small the difference — it is a difference detector, not a magnitude meter.
questions.parallel_form_stem: In a different unbalanced bridge, the top midpoint reads 5.0 V and the bottom reads 4.5 V. State what the galvanometer does and why.

questions.stem: A Wheatstone bridge has resistor P = 10 Ω, resistor Q = 20 Ω, resistor S = 6 Ω. At what value of resistor R does the galvanometer read exactly zero, and what does that null actually mean?

options.A: 3 Ω — at this value the ratio of the top arms equals the ratio of the bottom arms, the two midpoints reach the same potential, and the galvanometer carries zero current even though all four arms keep flowing
options.B: 6 Ω — at this value all four resistors are equal, so the bridge balances
options.C: 3 Ω, and at this setting every current in the circuit, including the arms, drops to zero
options.D: There is no value of resistor R that gives exactly zero — the needle can only get close to centre

distractor_misconceptions.B: the equal-resistor trap — balance is a RATIO match, not a requirement that all four arms share the same value (here 10 Ω and 20 Ω are deliberately unequal)
distractor_misconceptions.C: the everything-stops trap — only the galvanometer branch reads zero at balance; the four arms keep carrying real current
distractor_misconceptions.D: wrongly denies that an exact null exists — the ratio condition is met EXACTLY at one value of R, not merely approached

questions.tested_idea: Balance is a RATIO match (top-arm ratio equals bottom-arm ratio) achieved at one specific R, giving exactly zero galvanometer current — not an equal-resistor condition, and not a circuit-wide current stop.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A bridge has resistor P = 5 Ω, resistor Q = 15 Ω, resistor S = 9 Ω. Find the value of resistor R that balances the bridge, and explain what the resulting zero reading does and does not mean.

questions.stem: At balance (resistor R = 3 Ω in the same bridge), the galvanometer reads exactly zero. What is true of the current in resistor P and in resistor R at that same instant?

options.A: Both still carry current — about 0.2 A through the top path and about 0.67 A through the bottom path — only the galvanometer branch is silent
options.B: Both currents are also zero, since the whole bridge is 'balanced'
options.C: Only the top path carries current; the bottom path shuts off entirely
options.D: The currents reverse direction back into the battery

distractor_misconceptions.B: the classic everything-stops trap — a zero GALVANOMETER reading does not mean a zero circuit; the four arms are still driven by the battery and keep flowing
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents a path shutting off with no physical mechanism — both divider paths remain complete, unbroken circuits
distractor_misconceptions.D: invents a reversal with no cause — the current direction in each arm is set by the battery and does not depend on the galvanometer branch

questions.tested_idea: At balance, only the bridge-wire current is zero — every arm keeps carrying its own steady current, set entirely by the battery and that arm's own resistors.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A different balanced bridge has a top-path current of 0.4 A and a bottom-path current of 0.9 A. State what the galvanometer reads, and whether the 0.4 A and 0.9 A currents are still flowing.

questions.stem: With the same balanced bridge (resistor P = 10 Ω, resistor Q = 20 Ω, resistor R = 3 Ω, resistor S = 6 Ω), the battery voltage is changed from 6 V to 10 V. What happens to the galvanometer reading and to the calculated value of resistor S?

options.A: The galvanometer stays at zero, and resistor S is still worked out the same way from resistor R and the ratio of resistor Q to resistor P — the battery voltage never enters that calculation
options.B: The galvanometer deflects, because a bigger battery should unbalance the bridge
options.C: The galvanometer stays at zero, but resistor S must now be recalculated using the new battery voltage
options.D: Resistor S changes to a new value because the arm currents changed

distractor_misconceptions.B: assumes the battery's SIZE affects the balance condition, when balance depends only on the resistor RATIO, not on how much current is actually flowing
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents a role for the battery voltage in a formula that, by construction, never contains it
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses the arm CURRENTS changing, which is true, with the arm RESISTANCE changing, which is false — resistor S is a fixed physical resistor, unaffected by the battery

questions.tested_idea: The null and the calculated resistor S = R × (Q/P) are both independent of the battery's voltage — the battery only sets the size of the (irrelevant, at balance) arm currents.
questions.parallel_form_stem: In a different balanced bridge, the battery is swapped from 9 V to 4.5 V. State what happens to the galvanometer reading and to the calculated unknown resistance.

questions.stem: A Wheatstone bridge has ratio arms resistor P = 100 Ω and resistor Q = 10 Ω. The galvanometer reads exactly zero when the known arm resistor R = 153 Ω. What is the unknown resistance, resistor S?

options.A: 15.3 Ω, from resistor R times the ratio of resistor Q to resistor P: 153 times ten over one hundred
options.B: 1530 Ω, from resistor R times the ratio of resistor P to resistor Q
options.C: 153 Ω, since resistor S must equal resistor R at balance
options.D: 63 Ω, from resistor R minus resistor P plus resistor Q

distractor_misconceptions.B: inverts the ratio — uses resistor P over resistor Q instead of resistor Q over resistor P, giving a value 100 times too large
distractor_misconceptions.C: the equal-arm trap again — balance never requires the unknown to equal the known arm, only that the RATIOS match
distractor_misconceptions.D: invents an arithmetic combination with no basis in the balance condition

questions.tested_idea: Resistor S equals resistor R times the ratio of resistor Q to resistor P at balance — the correct ratio orientation, not its inverse, and not a simple equality with resistor R.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A bridge has ratio arms resistor P = 40 Ω and resistor Q = 5 Ω, balancing at known arm resistor R = 96 Ω. Find the unknown resistance, resistor S.

regeneration_variants.label: A digital weighing scale's hidden strain-gauge bridge
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A digital weighing scale hides this exact bridge inside it — a strain gauge, a resistor whose value shifts a hair when the platform flexes under your weight, sits as one arm. Stepping on unbalances the bridge, and that tiny imbalance becomes the weight reading.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same P/Q = R/S balance identity — the scale's electronics detect a hair-thin resistance change no ordinary meter could see, exactly the same ratio-match sensitivity the sim's null method demonstrates.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Primary teaching anchor (real_world_anchor.primary) restated as a Type-B variant for STATE_1's topology hook and STATE_4's precision beat.

regeneration_variants.label: Temperature and pressure sensors reading a tiny resistance shift
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Many temperature and pressure sensors use the same bridge to turn a tiny resistance shift — caused by heat or force — into an electrical signal, the same way the sim's unknown arm S is found by nulling the galvanometer.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same balance-ratio identity P/Q = R/S, now with the sensing element as one arm instead of an unknown lab resistor.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Narration-only secondary anchor for STATE_4's generalization beat; nothing sensor-shaped is drawn on canvas.
